One of the best parts of writing Not Boring is that I get to send out a bat=

When I wrote Bill-a-Bear [https://email.mg2.substack.com/c/eJxFkMuOhCAQRb9G=

y, and mentioned my displeasure with bill.com, a couple things happened. Fi=
rst, a bunch of people replied saying that I should check out Melio. Second=
, Melio=E2=80=99s co-founder and CEO, Matan Bar [https://email.mg2.substack=
=2Ecom/c/eJxFkM2OhCAQhJ9mOBpoBPXAYS_zGoa=
f1iGjYLDdiW-_OB42gSakUhT1eUs453KaLe_E=

hTU], reached out to tell me about what his team is building, and why. Turn=
s out, I wasn=E2=80=99t alone. Small businesses don=E2=80=99t want complica=
ted software with automations and bells and whistles. They just want to pay=

I immediately resonated with Melio=E2=80=99s product, and it=E2=80=99s stra=

Disruptive innovation:=C2=A0
Describes a process whereby a smaller company with fewer resources is able =
to successfully challenge established incumbent businesses.=C2=A0
Specifically, as incumbents focus on improving their products and services =
for their most demanding (and usually most profitable) customers, they exce=
ed the needs of some segments and ignore the needs of others.=C2=A0
Entrants that prove disruptive begin by successfully targeting those overlo=
oked segments, gaining a foothold by delivering more-suitable functionality=
=E2=80=94frequently at a lower price.
Incumbents, chasing higher profitability in more-demanding segments, tend n=
ot to respond vigorously.=C2=A0
Entrants then move upmarket, delivering the performance that incumbents=E2=
=80=99 mainstream customers require, while preserving the advantages that d=
rove their early success.=C2=A0
When mainstream customers start adopting the entrants=E2=80=99 offerings in=
 volume, disruption has occurred.
They proposed two types of disruption:
New-market disruption targets under-served customers who weren=E2=80=99t co=
nsuming before because they lacked the money, skill, access, ability, or ti=
me to consume existing offerings.=C2=A0
Low-end disruption targets over-served customers by paring down products an=
d offering them to consumers at lower prices.=C2=A0
It=E2=80=99s important to keep in mind that the incumbent typically isn=E2=
=80=99t stupid or unaware. They see the low-end of the market, and they mak=
e an intentional strategic choice to focus on their existing customers and =
the more profitable ones further upmarket. Their goals and incentives are a=
ligned with driving profitability today, and they=E2=80=99re willing to tak=
e the chance that a new entrant is able to both figure out how to serve the=
 low-end profitably, and then come upmarket. Disruption is a long process.=
=C2=A0=C2=A0
But when it works, it works, and it looks obvious in hindsight. Disruption =
is one of the most-used concepts in business strategy because it=E2=80=99s =
the playbook that new companies can use to topple incumbents, kind of like =
Bill. Melio, it turns out, is an example of both new-market disruption and,=
 increasingly, low-end disruption.=C2=A0

d listen to the full episode):
Even the name of the category -- Accounts Payables Automation Solutions -- =
that=E2=80=99s such a terrible name for a category. Accounts Payable is an =
accounting term, and Automation is this value proposition that probably res=
onates really well with the CFO of Nike. However, to a wine shop owner that=
 manages 100 payments a month, automation is not that big of a deal=E2=80=
=A6 what these businesses care about is cashflow, not automation.=C2=A0
Targeting the low-end of the market means so much more than just which valu=
e proposition to highlight, it means building everything completely differe=
ntly, from the ground up.=C2=A0
The economics are different. A wine shop with no finance team is very diffe=
rent from a Widget Corporation with finance, accounting, and purchasing tea=
ms that buy millions of widget parts. Each Melio customer sends fewer payme=
nts and spends less money, which changes how Melio need to approach the top=
 and bottom line. Onboarding happens directly in the product. There=E2=80=
=99s no =E2=80=9CSchedule a Demo,=E2=80=9D no manual credit checks or risk =
assessments. The economics don=E2=80=99t support that.=C2=A0
What seems like a constraint can be an advantage, though. Melio needed to b=
uild great software to do all of the things that a company targeting larger=
 customers can pay to do manually.=C2=A0
As one example, Matan told me that early on, when Melio started advertising=
 on Facebook, scammers started signing up and making a lot of transactions.=
 They looked like great customers, because they signed up and transacted a =
lot, right away, so Facebook served them more scammers. They had to choose =
between cutting off that channel and building fraud detection right into th=
e signup flow to catch them before they could do any damage. They chose the=
 latter. That=E2=80=99s something that Melio can do as a modern tech compan=
y that Bill couldn=E2=80=99t with a more manual onboarding.=C2=A0
Those constraints also force focus.=C2=A0
Instead of aiming for full feature parity with the incumbents, including au=
tomation, Melio focused on the things that are important to small businesse=
s: self-serve onboarding, a consumer-like experience, and most importantly,=
 cashflow management.
Because Melio spends less to acquire and onboard customers, and because sof=
tware handles the vast majority of the work to serve them, Melio has low ma=
rginal costs and can offer a lower price -- free, in many cases. Customers =
can use Melio to send and receive payments for free. I=E2=80=99ve collected=
 tens of thousands of dollars through Melio, and I haven=E2=80=99t paid a c=
ent.=C2=A0
Instead, Melio makes money by offering its customers products that improve =
cashflow. Melio customers can pay a couple of dollars to send payments to v=
endors more quickly than ACH, for example. Sending payments in one day inst=
ead of five means that you can keep cash in your bank account for four extr=
a days. Vendors can also pay to receive money more quickly.
In any business, particularly SMBs, cash is king. A company can improve its=
 cash position without doing a dollar more in sales or cutting any costs by=
 stretching out payables and pulling in receivables. There=E2=80=99s even a=
 formula for it: the Cash Conversion Cycle formula.=C2=A0
Shortening the time it takes to collect money (DSO) or increasing the time =
it takes to pay money (DPO) both improve the cash conversion cycle. Melio t=
akes risk to make it possible -- one of the biggest loss-drivers for this k=
ind of business is fronting a customer money and getting hit with an =E2=80=
=9CInsufficient Funds=E2=80=9D when they try to collect -- and gets a fee i=
n exchange.=C2=A0=C2=A0
To make that possible, Melio uses sophisticated risk and credit models to a=
utomatically underwrite any business=E2=80=99 likelihood of payment. The na=
me of the game is extending more credit while taking less risk.
Helping payers and vendors improve their cash conversion cycle isn=E2=80=99=
t just good for revenue, though. It=E2=80=99s phenomenal for growth. And Me=
lio is growing fast.=C2=A0

nd Melio=E2=80=99s future=E2=80=A6=20

1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">After writing=
 that piece, I got two types of responses, from two groups of people:</p><o=

ont-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">Turns out that there=

low-end disruption in B2B payments. SMBs need a smoother, more modern, and =
more intuitive B2B payments solution, at a lower price, to move off of pape=
r checks or the bank ACH process.</p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size:=
 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">That’s exactly what Mel=

height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">Melio, which launched less than two years=

/li><li style=3D"margin: 3.75px 0 3.75px 7.5px; mso-special-format: bullet;=
"><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0=
;">Entrants that prove disruptive <strong>begin by successfully targeting t=
hose overlooked segments</strong>, gaining a foothold by <strong>delivering=
 more-suitable functionality</strong>—<strong>frequently at a lower p=
rice</strong>.</p></li><li style=3D"margin: 3.75px 0 3.75px 7.5px; mso-spec=
ial-format: bullet;"><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-heig=
ht: 26px; margin: 0;">Incumbents, <strong>chasing higher profitability in m=
ore-demanding segments</strong>, tend not to respond vigorously. </p><=
/li><li style=3D"margin: 3.75px 0 3.75px 7.5px; mso-special-format: bullet;=
"><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0=
;"><strong>Entrants then move upmarket</strong>, delivering the performance=
 that incumbents’ mainstream customers require, <strong>while preserv=
ing the advantages that drove their early success</strong>. </p></li><=
li style=3D"margin: 3.75px 0 3.75px 7.5px; mso-special-format: bullet;"><p =
style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0;"><s=
trong>When mainstream customers start adopting the entrants’ offering=
s in volume, disruption has occurred.</strong></p></li></ul><p style=3D"col=
or: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">They p=
roposed two types of disruption:</p><ul style=3D"margin: 1em 0;"><li style=
=3D"margin: 3.75px 0 3.75px 7.5px; mso-special-format: bullet;"><p style=3D=
"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0;"><em><stron=
g>New-market disruption </strong>targets under-served customers who weren&#=
8217;t consuming before because they lacked the money, skill, access, abili=
ty, or time to consume existing offerings. </em></p></li><li style=3D"=
margin: 3.75px 0 3.75px 7.5px; mso-special-format: bullet;"><p style=3D"col=
or: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0;"><strong>Low-en=
d disruption</strong> targets over-served customers by paring down products=
 and offering them to consumers at lower prices. </p></li></ul><p styl=
e=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;=
">It’s important to keep in mind that the incumbent typically isn&#82=
17;t stupid or unaware. They see the low-end of the market, and they make a=
n intentional strategic choice to focus on their existing customers and the=
 more profitable ones further upmarket. Their goals and incentives are alig=
ned with driving profitability today, and they’re willing to take the=
 chance that a new entrant is able to both figure out how to serve the low-=
end profitably, and <em>then</em> come upmarket. Disruption is a long proce=
ss.  </p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height=
: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">But when it works, it works, and it looks obvio=
us in hindsight. Disruption is one of the most-used concepts in business st=
rategy because it’s the playbook that new companies can use to topple=
 incumbents, kind of like Bill. Melio, it turns out, is an example of both =
new-market disruption and, increasingly, low-end disruption. </p><p st=
yle=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em =
0;">When I wrote <em>Bill-A-Bear, </em>I wrote about the way a competitor m=
ight topple Bill:</p><blockquote style=3D"border-left: 4px solid #67bdfc; m=
argin: 1em 0;"><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26=
px; margin: 0 0 1em 0; margin-left: 1em;"><em>Bill.com has left a wide open=
ing for new entrants to come in and build better AR/AP software that makes =
getting paid fun and helps companies better manage their cash conversion cy=
cles. </em></p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-heigh=
t: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0; margin-left: 1em;"><em>To knock off bill.com, S=
quare, Stripe, Settle, or a new, product-first competitor will need to:</em=
></p></blockquote><ol style=3D"margin: 1em 0;"><li style=3D"margin: 3.75px =
0 3.75px 7.5px; mso-special-format: bullet;"><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; fo=
nt-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0;"><em>Build a seamless product =
to feature parity with bill.com on the things that matter to customers.&nbs=
p;</em></p></li><li style=3D"margin: 3.75px 0 3.75px 7.5px; mso-special-for=
mat: bullet;"><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26p=
x; margin: 0;"><em>Build distribution into the product workflows. </em=
></p></li><li style=3D"margin: 3.75px 0 3.75px 7.5px; mso-special-format: b=
ullet;"><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; mar=
gin: 0;"><em>Build up enough customer demand to attract enough vendors and =
partners onto the platform that Bill’s switching costs and network ef=
fects are rendered obsolete.  </em></p></li></ol><p style=3D"colo=
r: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">I wrote=
 that from the perspective of a well-funded incumbent like Stripe or Square=
=2E I thought it would be very difficult=
 for a new company to go from 0 to 10=
0 to win the market. I underestimated just how big and unserved the SMB seg=
ment was.</p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px=
; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">As I wrote, unbeknownst to me, a group of people in T=
el Aviv and NYC were building a product with the features that mattered mos=
t to their target customers, with virality built in, growing like a weed.&n=
bsp;</p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; mar=
gin: 0 0 1em 0;">It’s just that Melio was still in stealth. </p>=
<h3 style=3D"-moz-osx-font-smoothing: antialiased; -webkit-font-smoothing: =
antialiased; color: #1a1a1a; font-family: 'SF Compact Display', -apple-syst=

; font-weight: bold; line-height: 1.16em; margin: 1em 0 0.625em 0;"><strong=
>Meet Melio</strong></h3><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-=

compliance, and B2B-grade workflows.</p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-si=
ze: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">To date, Melio has mainly =
focused on Accounts Payable (AP) and Accounts Receivable (AR), helping smal=
l businesses pay or get paid in whatever way works best -- ACH, debit card,=
 they’ll even mail physical checks. And they do it all for free. They=
 only charge when they help businesses improve their cashflow by sending or=
 receiving money more quickly, or by using a credit card. </p><p style=
=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;"=
>Melio is a product born of deep customer empathy and time spent on the lig=
ht side of the payments industry: consumer. </p><p style=3D"color: #1a=
1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">Matan Bar has=
 been in the payments space for a long time. In 2009, he co-founded The Gif=
ts Project, a group payments platform. eBay acquired the company in 2012, a=
nd Matan became the GM of the company’s Israel Innovation Center. In =
2015, when eBay spun off PayPal, Matan went with the payments giant, where =
he led Global P2P Payments, working on PayPal’s own app and its Venmo=
 subsidiary. </p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-hei=
ght: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">Consumer was a great place to start. Peer-to=
-Peer (P2P) payments are further along than B2B payments. Think about the l=
ast time you paid a friend, and the last time your business paid a vendor. =
They’re entirely different. Venmo takes a second. Paying vendors can =
take weeks, or longer. Venmo is fun, bill.com is… not fun. And the pe=
ople who use bill.com are the lucky ones. Most companies still write checks=
 or send payments via ACH and wait days. </p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1=
a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">B2B payments are=
 also much, much bigger than consumer. Globally, all P2P payments are expec=
ted to reach about <a href=3D"https://email.mg2.substack.com/c/eJxFUcuu4yAM=

</p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin:=
 0 0 1em 0;">But while small business owners use Venmo to pay their friends=

e-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">“Being part of the flow is better=

ed up 40 nuances we couldn’t have discovered by asking questions, bec=
ause we didn’t even know which questions to ask!” </p><p s=
tyle=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em=
 0;">Hard-won insights in hand, they got to building. In mid-2019, they lau=
nched in NYC, starting with wine shops. </p><h3 style=3D"-moz-osx-font=
-smoothing: antialiased; -webkit-font-smoothing: antialiased; color: #1a1a1=
a; font-family: 'SF Compact Display', -apple-system, BlinkMacSystemFont, 'S=

 UI Emoji', 'Segoe UI Symbol'; font-size: 1.375em; font-weight: bold; line-=
height: 1.16em; margin: 1em 0 0.625em 0;"><strong>Started From the Bottom</=
strong></h3><blockquote style=3D"border-left: 4px solid #67bdfc; margin: 1e=
m 0;"><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margi=
n: 0 0 1em 0; margin-left: 1em;"><em>As incumbents <strong>focus on improvi=
ng their products and services for their most demanding (and usually most p=
rofitable) customers</strong>, they <strong>exceed the needs of some segmen=
ts and ignore the needs of others</strong>. Entrants that prove disruptive =
<strong>begin by successfully targeting those overlooked segments</strong>,=
 gaining a foothold by <strong>delivering more-suitable functionality</stro=
ng>—<strong>frequently at a lower price</strong>.</em></p></blockquot=
e><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0=
 0 1em 0;">The first rule of disruption is, intuitively, to start at the lo=
w-end, the part of the market that incumbents overserve and overlook as the=
y chase bigger, more profitable customers. Melio isn’t stealing custo=
mers from bill.com yet. It doesn’t need to. </p><p style=3D"colo=
r: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">Bill.co=
m is focused on mid-sized businesses, and it’s moving upmarket. It ha=
s guided investors to expect that it will sign up fewer but larger customer=
s. Classic incumbent move. This is one of the most fascinating things about=
 disruption -- the moves that the incumbent makes often make sense. If Bill=
 can acquire larger customers, it will generate more revenue, more profitab=
ly over the next few years. What CEO wouldn’t want that? </p><p =
style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1e=
m 0;">But it does leave the low-end of the market wide open for Melio.</p><=
p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 =
1em 0;">Melio started with wine shops, creators, makers, and crafters, smal=
l businesses doing tens or hundreds of thousands or low millions of dollars=
 in revenue. Many of these businesses were still paying paper invoices with=
 paper checks, even though bill.com has been around since 2006, and Tipalti=
 since 2010. This is <strong>new-market disruption</strong>, serving previo=
us non-consumers. </p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; lin=
e-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">If you want to understand why they didn=
’t consume existing tech solutions, look no further than the category=
 name: <strong>Accounts Payable Automation Solutions</strong>. </p><p =
style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1e=
m 0;">Matan <a href=3D"https://email.mg2.substack.com/c/eJxFkclOwzAQhp8mvhF=
5ibMcfACxqNBDoRy4RV6mqWliR7YDhKfHbQ9Is2lGo3_0jZYJBh9WMfuY0Dn0aZ1BOPiOI6QEAS=
0RQm-NIDVtGa86ZERlSMtbZGN_CACTtKNIYQE0L2q0Wibr3WUDY8rRUYAhWnEsGw1Ed51sFcbQV=
Tj3uZaEX3XlYiw4DQK-IKzeARrFMaU5Fuy2oI_Z_AyujLNP9rCW2k-5BbON3kCuyP3LW4c_NQRg=
9SY09WnztP0t2GO0Bbv_eV5P-CZ8vA_TLj3stvu7ffP-iqygmBLMaUMY5xUrSck0k7rVB3rQCnR=
jsndaKUW7hlaqkkWFp4GWcVExSX0634GCcD4pH6wb8vh0lBOMl0EG0uc8Lc6mtQcn1QjmyipdkV=
_o9QM4CPkVppfpDJoSyhqeY3Nlk2GyusOE1jXKysbnLfcv-gfbb5gx" style=3D"color: #1a=
1a1a; text-decoration: underline;">told Harry Stebbings</a> (and you should=
 listen to the full episode):</p><blockquote style=3D"border-left: 4px soli=
d #67bdfc; margin: 1em 0;"><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; lin=
e-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0; margin-left: 1em;"><em>Even the name of =
the category -- Accounts Payables Automation Solutions -- that’s such=
 a terrible name for a category. Accounts Payable is an accounting term, an=
d Automation is this value proposition that probably resonates really well =
with the CFO of Nike. However, to a wine shop owner that manages 100 paymen=
ts a month, automation is not that big of a deal… what these business=
es care about is <strong>cashflow</strong>, not automation. </em></p><=
/blockquote><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px;=
 margin: 0 0 1em 0;">Targeting the low-end of the market means so much more=
 than just which value proposition to highlight, it means building everythi=
ng completely differently, from the ground up. </p><p style=3D"color: =
#1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">The econom=
ics are different. A wine shop with no finance team is very different from =
a Widget Corporation with finance, accounting, and purchasing teams that bu=
y millions of widget parts. Each Melio customer sends fewer payments and sp=
ends less money, which changes how Melio need to approach the top and botto=
m line. Onboarding happens directly in the product. There’s no &#8220=
;Schedule a Demo,” no manual credit checks or risk assessments. The e=
conomics don’t support that. </p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; fon=
t-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">What seems like a cons=
traint can be an advantage, though. Melio needed to build great software to=
 do all of the things that a company targeting larger customers can pay to =
do manually. </p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-hei=
ght: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">As one example, Matan told me that early on,=
 when Melio started advertising on Facebook, scammers started signing up an=
d making a lot of transactions. They looked like great customers, because t=
hey signed up and transacted a lot, right away, so Facebook served them <em=
>more </em>scammers. They had to choose between cutting off that channel an=
d building fraud detection right into the signup flow to catch them before =
they could do any damage. They chose the latter. That’s something tha=
t Melio can do as a modern tech company that Bill couldn’t with a mor=
e manual onboarding. </p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; =
line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">Those constraints also force focus.&=
nbsp;</p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; ma=
rgin: 0 0 1em 0;">Instead of aiming for full feature parity with the incumb=
ents, including automation, Melio focused on the things that are important =
to small businesses: self-serve onboarding, a consumer-like experience, and=
 most importantly, cashflow management.</p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font=
-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">Because Melio spends le=
ss to acquire and onboard customers, and because software handles the vast =
majority of the work to serve them, Melio has low marginal costs and can of=
fer a lower price -- free, in many cases. Customers can use Melio to send a=
nd receive payments for free. I’ve collected tens of thousands of dol=
lars through Melio, and I haven’t paid a cent. </p><div class=3D=

26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">Shortening the time it takes to collect money (DS=

 conversion cycle. Melio takes risk to make it possible -- one of the bigge=

getting hit with an “Insufficient Funds” when they try to colle=
ct -- and gets a fee in exchange.  </p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a=
; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">To make that poss=
ible, Melio uses sophisticated risk and credit models to automatically unde=
rwrite any business’ likelihood of payment. The name of the game is e=
xtending more credit while taking less risk.</p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a;=
 font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">Helping payers and=
